template: What is the name of the model that describes how an enzyme and its substrate interact?
explanation: The lock and key model describes how a substrate (key) fits precisely into the complementary active site of an enzyme (lock). Only a substrate with the correct shape can bind.

template: At what approximate temperature does most human enzyme activity peak?
explanation: Human enzymes have an optimum temperature of approximately 37°C (body temperature). Above this, the enzyme denatures as the active site changes shape.

template: Explain what happens to an enzyme when it is denatured.

answerConfig.textAnswer: the shape of the active site changes so the substrate can no longer bind
acceptedAnswers: substrate no longer fits the active site
the enzyme loses its shape and stops working

explanation: Denaturation causes the bonds holding the enzyme's 3D structure to break. The active site changes shape permanently so the substrate can no longer bind — the enzyme stops working.

template: Where in the digestive system does amylase work best?
explanation: Amylase works optimally at neutral/slightly alkaline pH (around pH 7–8). It is produced in the salivary glands (mouth) and pancreas, and works in the mouth and small intestine.

template: Match each enzyme to the substrate it breaks down.
explanation: Amylase breaks down starch into simple sugars. Protease breaks down proteins into amino acids. Lipase breaks down lipids (fats) into fatty acids and glycerol.

template: How many chambers does the human heart have?
explanation: The human heart has four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, and left ventricle. The right side pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs; the left side pumps oxygenated blood to the body.

template: Match each blood vessel to its correct description.

pairs.right: Thick muscular wall, carries blood away from heart
Has valves, carries blood towards heart
One cell thick wall, site of exchange

explanation: Arteries carry blood away from the heart under high pressure — thick muscular walls. Veins carry blood back to the heart under low pressure — have valves to prevent backflow. Capillaries are one cell thick to allow exchange of substances.

template: What is meant by a double circulatory system?

answerConfig.textAnswer: blood passes through the heart twice for every complete circuit of the body
acceptedAnswers: two separate circuits: one to the lungs and one to the body
blood goes to lungs then returns to heart before going to the body

explanation: A double circulatory system has two loops: pulmonary circulation (heart → lungs → heart) and systemic circulation (heart → body → heart). Blood passes through the heart twice per circuit, maintaining high pressure to the body.

template: Select ALL features of alveoli that make them efficient for gas exchange.
explanation: Alveoli are efficient because they have: large surface area (many alveoli), thin walls (one cell thick) for short diffusion distance, rich blood supply to maintain concentration gradient, moist lining for gases to dissolve.

template: What reagent is used to test for the presence of starch?
explanation: Iodine solution turns blue-black in the presence of starch. If no starch is present, the solution remains orange-brown.

template: What colour does Benedict's solution turn when glucose is present after heating?
explanation: Benedict's solution turns brick red/orange when heated with a reducing sugar such as glucose. A negative result remains blue.

template: Select ALL conditions that would increase the rate of transpiration.
explanation: Transpiration increases with: higher temperature (more evaporation), higher light intensity (stomata open wider), wind (removes water vapour maintaining gradient). High humidity reduces the concentration gradient so slows transpiration.

template: Match each tissue to what it transports.

pairs.right: Water and mineral ions upward from roots
Sucrose and amino acids in both directions

explanation: Xylem transports water and dissolved mineral ions from roots to leaves (transpiration stream) — movement is one-way upward. Phloem transports sucrose and amino acids from leaves to other parts (translocation) — movement is bidirectional.

template: Explain how fatty deposits cause coronary heart disease.

answerConfig.textAnswer: fatty deposits narrow the coronary arteries reducing blood flow to the heart muscle causing a heart attack
acceptedAnswers: fatty plaques build up in coronary arteries restricting blood flow to heart
atherosclerosis narrows arteries reducing oxygen supply to heart muscle

explanation: Fatty deposits (plaques/atherosclerosis) build up inside the walls of coronary arteries, narrowing the lumen. This reduces blood flow and oxygen supply to the heart muscle. If a clot blocks the artery completely, a heart attack results.

template: What is the role of the pacemaker in the heart?

answerConfig.textAnswer: it controls the resting heart rate by producing electrical impulses
acceptedAnswers: sets the rhythm of the heartbeat
generates electrical signals that cause the heart to contract

explanation: The natural pacemaker (sinoatrial node) in the right atrium generates electrical impulses that spread across the heart, causing the atria and then ventricles to contract in a coordinated rhythm.

template: A student measures enzyme activity at different substrate concentrations. At very high substrate concentrations the rate stops increasing. Explain why.

answerConfig.textAnswer: all active sites are occupied so increasing substrate concentration has no further effect
acceptedAnswers: the enzyme is saturated — all active sites are full
no more active sites available for substrate to bind
every active site is occupied with substrate

explanation: At very high substrate concentrations all enzyme active sites are occupied at all times — the enzyme is saturated. Adding more substrate cannot increase the rate as there are no free active sites available.

template: What are the products when lipase breaks down a fat molecule?
explanation: Lipase catalyses the hydrolysis of lipids (fats/oils) into fatty acids and glycerol. These smaller molecules can then be absorbed through the gut wall.

template: State TWO functions of bile in digestion.
explanation: Bile has two main roles: (1) emulsifying fats — breaking large fat droplets into smaller ones increasing surface area for lipase; (2) neutralising stomach acid — bile is alkaline, creating the optimum pH for enzymes in the small intestine.

template: Explain how villi are adapted for the absorption of digested food.

answerConfig.markScheme: 1. Large surface area for absorption (1) / 2. Single layer of epithelial cells — short diffusion distance (1) / 3. Rich blood supply / capillary network maintains concentration gradient (1) / 4. Lacteals absorb fatty acids and glycerol (1) / 5. Microvilli further increase surface area (1) — max 4

explanation: Villi are finger-like projections lining the small intestine. They have a large surface area, a single layer of cells (short diffusion distance), a rich capillary network (maintains concentration gradient for glucose/amino acids), and lacteals for fat absorption.

template: What is the function of stomata in a leaf?

answerConfig.textAnswer: they allow gas exchange — carbon dioxide in and oxygen out — and water vapour to leave
acceptedAnswers: allow CO2 in and O2 out

explanation: Stomata are pores in the leaf surface that allow gas exchange (CO2 in for photosynthesis, O2 out) and water vapour loss (transpiration). They are opened and closed by guard cells.

template: Order the path of deoxygenated blood through the heart and lungs.
explanation: Deoxygenated blood enters the right atrium → right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs (gas exchange) → pulmonary vein → left atrium → left ventricle → aorta → body.

template: What is the product when protease breaks down a protein?
explanation: Protease enzymes catalyse the hydrolysis of proteins into their constituent amino acids, which can then be absorbed and used to build new proteins.

template: What is the product of amylase breaking down starch?
explanation: Amylase catalyses the hydrolysis of starch into maltose (a disaccharide). Maltase then further breaks maltose into glucose.

template: Explain how the structure of an enzyme allows it to catalyse a specific reaction. Include reference to the active site.

answerConfig.markScheme: 1. Enzyme has a specific active site shape (1) / 2. Active site is complementary to a specific substrate (1) / 3. Substrate binds to active site forming enzyme-substrate complex (1) / 4. Only one substrate fits due to complementary shape — specificity (1) / 5. Changing pH/temperature changes active site shape so substrate cannot bind (1) — max 4

explanation: Each enzyme has a uniquely shaped active site that is complementary to one specific substrate. When the substrate binds, an enzyme-substrate complex forms and the reaction is catalysed. This explains enzyme specificity — only the correct substrate fits.

template: Match each plant organ to its function.
explanation: Leaves are the main site of photosynthesis. Stems provide support and contain xylem and phloem. Roots absorb water and minerals and anchor the plant. Flowers are reproductive organs.
